metadata.title: Terminal Value Is Driven by g, Not WACC

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Seeing EV nearly triple as g moves from 1.5 to 4.5 percent while a WACC row change barely halves it convinced me terminal value is a g story. I want to expose this on my own model. Help me: (1) build the WACC-versus-g sensitivity grid and confirm what fraction of enterprise value sits in the terminal value; (2) render it as a heat map so the dominant driver is visually obvious; (3) run a bull g=4% versus bear g=1.5% case and show the valuation range one analyst's assumption can justify.

props.command: Seeing EV nearly triple as g moves from 1.5 to 4.5 percent while a WACC row change barely halves it convinced me terminal value is a g story. I want to expose this on my own model. Help me: (1) build the WACC-versus-g sensitivity grid and confirm what fraction of enterprise value sits in the terminal value; (2) render it as a heat map so the dominant driver is visually obvious; (3) run a bull g=4% versus bear g=1.5% case and show the valuation range one analyst's assumption can justify.
props.segment: Terminal Value Is Driven by g,

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

lines: g drives it. WACC does not.

beats.narration_text: A typical DCF assigns 60 to 80 percent of enterprise value to the terminal value. The terminal value is determined almost entirely by one number: g, the perpetual growth rate.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- terminal value = 60-80% of DCF. g is the dominant driver.

beats.narration_text: Can you see this sensitivity before you trust a single point estimate?

props.command: claude "Build a DCF sensitivity grid: FCFF=[120,135,148,160,170]M, terminal value = 170*(1+g)/(WACC-g). Compute EV for WACC in [7%,8%,9%,10%] and g in [1.5%,2.5%,3.5%,4.5%]. Print a 4x4 table."

beats.narration_text: The script builds a WACC-versus-g grid, computing enterprise value for each pair and printing a sensitivity table.

props.code:
FCFF=[120,135,148,160,170]
WACC_vals=[0.07,0.08,0.09,0.10]
g_vals=[0.015,0.025,0.035,0.045]
TV_FCFF=170
for wacc in WACC_vals:
    row=[]
    for g in g_vals:
        TV=TV_FCFF*(1+g)/(wacc-g)
        PV_FCFF=sum(f/(1+wacc)**(i+1)
            for i,f in enumerate(FCFF))
        EV=PV_FCFF+TV/(1+wacc)**5
        row.append(int(EV))
    print(f"WACC={wacc*100:.0f}%: {row}")

beats.narration_text: The heat map fills in. Notice the rightmost column: EV nearly triples as g moves from 1.5 to 4.5 percent. Moving down a WACC row shrinks EV by at most half. g is the dominant driver.
beats.visual_intent: Manim B04_DCFSensitivity: 4x4 heat-map grid of EV values colored by tier.

beats.narration_text: Notice the rightmost column — EV nearly triples as g moves from 1.5 to 4.5 percent. Moving down a WACC row shrinks EV by at most half. g is the dominant driver.

props.command: claude "Now highlight: which input moves EV more? Compute EV range across g (fix WACC=8%) vs. EV range across WACC (fix g=2.5%). Print the ranges and the ratio."

beats.narration_text: Every CFO presenting one DCF number without this grid is hiding the real source of uncertainty.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- g-axis range 3.1x larger than WACC-axis range. The grid is the honest answer.

beats.narration_text: The teardown: this is not pessimism. It is honesty about which assumption drives value.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- recap: g is the dominant driver. Showing sensitivity is the discipline, not a flaw.

beats.narration_text: Re-run with bull g=4% vs. bear g=1.5%. One analyst's g assumption can justify undervalued or overvalued for the same firm.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- next steps: bull vs. bear g assumption. Same WACC, same firm, opposite conclusions.
